text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy, introducing instruments through a supraumbilical port to address a large, approximately twelve-centimeter myoma. Irrigation and suction are used, and a hanging drop test is conducted.

The surgeon uses a supraumbilical port to insert the laparoscopic telescope, positioned at least ten centimeters above the umbilicus to avoid interference from the uterine manipulator or myoma screw. The preset pressure is set to fifteen with a flow rate of one liter per minute. The trocar is inserted, preparing for the introduction of the telescope.

The surgeon identifies the plane of dissection around the intramural myoma, noting the marble white appearance of the uterus due to vasopressin. A myoma screw is applied to the apex of the uterus to stabilize the myoma. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel in the right hand to cut the serous layer in a crescent shape, facilitated by the ipsilateral port setup, while the assistant uses a contralateral port to hold the myoma screw.
The surgeon makes the initial incision on the uterus to access the large intramural myoma. The myoma is visibly protruding, facilitating its separation from the surrounding uterine muscle tissue.

The surgeon makes a concave incision on the myoma using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring to cut only the muscle and serosa without damaging the myoma itself. The myoma visibly protrudes from the uterine tissue as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw in the left hand and suction irrigation in the right hand to enucleate the myoma. The myoma is gradually separated from the base with gentle traction, avoiding abrupt evulsions to prevent opening the uterine cavity. Approximately ninety percent of the myoma is extracted, and the remaining layer is separated using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon identifies and coagulates any bleeders using bipolar or ligature after separating the uterus. The entire myoma is now removed, and the surgeon begins intracorporeal continuous suturing of the muscle layer in a triple-layer technique to approximate the muscle and stop bleeding.

The surgeon tightens the initial suture on the left side, with the assistant holding it using a contralateral port to provide traction. As two to three bites are taken, the bleeding visibly stops, allowing for continued suturing.

The surgeon observes minor bleeding collecting over the bowel and omentum, appearing larger due to laparoscopic magnification. Continuous suturing of the inner muscle layers is performed, starting from the right side and moving to the left, effectively approximating the muscle layers and stopping the bleeding.

The surgeon completes the inner muscle suturing, reaching the opposite end and extracting the needle from the serosa. The outer layer suturing begins using the same suture, with the Maryland forceps holding the tissue. The surgeon reverses direction, continuing the suturing from the left side back to the right side, tightening the outer layer.

The surgeon tightens the second layer of sutures, reaching the right end of the incision. The sutures are pulled taut, and the process involves returning back slightly to terminate the suturing with the same tail used initially.

The surgeon performs the termination of the second layer of suturing using a Dundee jamming knot, reinforcing the layer with continuous suturing. There is no visible bleeding, and the muscle is closed under pressure. The surgeon uses an interlocking suture technique, threading the needle through the loop and locking it, while maintaining traction with the left hand and advancing with the right hand.

The surgeon performs interlocking continuous suturing on the uterine muscle, taking bites between previous sutures without using a uterine manipulator. The suturing technique involves locking each stitch to reinforce the muscle layer.

The surgeon continues interlocking suturing on the outer layer of the uterine muscle, using the tail of the previous layer's suture for termination. The left hand holds the suture while the right hand uses a needle holder to tighten it. The uterus appears well-shaped with a slight bulge, which is expected to resolve over time. The patient has a single large myoma visible.

The surgeon completes the termination of the outer suture with a surgical knot, ensuring all needles are carefully removed. Suction irrigation is performed, revealing no significant bleeding in the uterus, as the initial surface bleeding has ceased.

The surgeon cuts the suture and removes the needle through a five-millimeter port. Final suction irrigation is performed to clean residual blood from the surgical field. The process of morselation begins, which involves fragmenting the large myoma for removal.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a morselator, highlighting the process of morselation within a MorSafe endo bag for safety. The procedure is performed through a twelve or fifteen millimeter port, which must be closed afterward to prevent hernia.
